parentQuestion: A patient develops sudden paralysis and loss of all sensation below the waist af...

scaffolds.q: The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord, including motor pathways, while the posterior spinal arteries supply the dorsal columns.
scaffolds.explain: Let me show you the vascular territories. The anterior spinal artery (single, midline) supplies the anterior and lateral regions of the cord — the corticospinal tracts (motor), spinothalamic tracts (pain/temperature), and anterior horns (motor neurons). The posterior spinal arteries (paired, posterolateral) supply the dorsal columns (proprioception/vibration). This vascular distribution creates a predictable infarct pattern: anterior spinal artery occlusion affects motor and pain/temperature, but spares proprioception and vibration. this is critical for recognizing spinal cord stroke.

scaffolds.q: Anterior spinal artery syndrome typically causes motor paralysis and loss of pain/temperature sensation below the lesion, but proprioception and vibration sense are preserved.
scaffolds.explain: Join me in examining this: and Witness this transformation:vascular dissection. Anterior spinal artery syndrome is a spinal cord stroke affecting the anterior two-thirds of the cord. The result: bilateral motor paralysis (corticospinal tracts dead), bilateral loss of pain/temperature (spinothalamic tracts dead), but preserved proprioception and vibration (dorsal columns spared by posterior spinal artery supply). Grant yourself this moment of clarity:into the clinical pearl: if a patient is paralyzed but can still tell you the position of their toes with their eyes closed, think anterior spinal artery syndrome. The vascular territory defines the deficit.
